episode.title: Contributor Onboarding — Ep 4 — Crew Check and Final Readiness

content.summary_short: The Crew Check, badge, and follow-up packet turn readiness into proof, but they do not replace scope review, access control, or human trust-building.
content.summary_long: The final step is to prove readiness in the same way the project expects real work to be proven: with explicit answers, clean proof, founder review, and a realistic first-scope request.

transcript: The Crew Check is the readiness gate for this onboarding flow. It tests whether a contributor can read the operating rules, understand the lanes, and choose the safe move when scope or risk changes.
The pass mark is eighty percent. Passing unlocks a badge and a follow-up packet that can be pasted into a discussion or issue for founder review.

That badge is not access control. It is a signal. The packet still needs practical details such as lane interest, availability, examples of work, and anything the founder should know before assigning real scope.

The safest first ask is small and reviewable. A contributor who asks for a narrow docs update, copy pass, JSON cleanup, or UI polish task is easier to trust than one asking to touch auth, deployment, secrets, or production data on day one.
This matters because proof is the pattern everywhere in AeroVista. The same mindset used for quizzes, work orders, and Definition of Done also applies to onboarding itself.
Final readiness means you understand the ecosystem, respect the boundaries, communicate honestly, and know that trust is built through clean proof instead of assumptions.

slides.title: Crew Check proves self-guidance
slides.body: The quiz measures rule-following, lane awareness, and self-guided judgment in realistic repo scenarios.
notes: The point is behavior, not trivia.
It checks whether the contributor can choose the safe move.

slides.title: Badge is signal, not access
slides.body: A pass creates a badge and proof packet, but permissions and real scope still come through review.
notes: Passing does not auto-grant repo or production access.
The badge helps the founder scan readiness quickly.
Signal is useful because it is grounded in explicit proof.

slides.title: Follow-up packet needs specifics
slides.body: The request packet should show lane interest, availability, links, and any relevant context for scope matching.
notes: Good follow-up still respects current needs and timing.
This is where alignment replaces assumption.

slides.title: Ready means safe and reviewable
slides.body: Contributor readiness is demonstrated the same way work quality is: with proof, boundaries, and repeatable behavior.
notes: Communicate honestly and leave reviewable proof behind.

slides.title: Ask for first scope wisely
slides.body: The best first task is narrow, low-risk, and easy to review. It proves judgment before permissions expand.
notes: Good first scope: docs, copy, styling polish, non-prod JSON, or clear QA notes.
Bad first scope: secrets, deployment, auth, billing, or production data.
Trust grows when the first request is modest and specific.

engage.prompt: What happens after a contributor passes Crew Check?

options.label: They unlock a badge and follow-up packet for founder review, but not automatic access.
They automatically gain access to restricted systems because the quiz replaces review.
They can skip work orders because the onboarding itself proves future scope.

final_assessment.intro: Pass every final check to record contributor onboarding readiness for this browser.

questions.name: co_final_q1
questions.prompt: A task touches auth or deployment pipelines. What is the correct handling?

options.label: Prepare the plan or safe patch, then escalate because founder executes the final Lane C change.
Ship it directly if the diff looks small.
Treat it as Lane A if the deadline is tight.

questions.name: co_final_q2
questions.prompt: What is the baseline for calling work done?

options.label: It works in preview, matches acceptance criteria, includes test notes, and includes a short docs note.
It runs locally on one machine.
It merged, so review details are optional.

questions.name: co_final_q3
questions.prompt: What should an Honest Helper ask for when the start point is unclear?

options.label: Source of truth, an example, and what done looks like.
Only a deadline, because speed matters most.
Permission to refactor broadly so the system makes sense.

questions.name: co_final_q4
questions.prompt: What does passing Crew Check grant?

options.label: A strong signal and a follow-up packet for review, not automatic access.
An exemption from work orders and review.

questions.name: co_final_q5
questions.prompt: What is the best first real task request after onboarding?

options.label: A narrow, low-risk task with clear files, acceptance criteria, and review path.
A broad refactor so the contributor can prove speed immediately.
A production credential or deployment change because the badge proves access.
